video_title: Babysitting the Machine: Glean's Rebecca Hinds on the Hidden Human Labor of AI at Work
gist: Rebecca Hinds of Glean's Work AI Institute presents the Work AI Index 2026 (n=6,000 digital workers), finding that while 87% of workers use AI and report saving 13 hours/week, only 13% say their organization is performing significantly better — a gap explained by 6.4 hours/week of hidden 'bot-sitting' labor and a 69% rate of 'bot-shitting' (shipping AI-generated work the employee cannot defend). The episode explores how context fragmentation, perverse incentives, and misalignment between individual productivity and team coordination drive the paradox, and what enterprise AI platforms (specifically Glean's enterprise graph) plus cultural interventions can do about it.

themes.theme: The AI Productivity Paradox: 13-hour savings vs. 13% org improvement

insights.claim: 87% of the 6,000 knowledge workers surveyed use AI, 73% say it makes them more productive, and on average they report saving 13 hours per week — roughly a third of a full work week — yet only 13% say their organization is performing significantly better as a result.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: The productivity-to-performance gap is the central puzzle of enterprise AI ROI; it suggests that individual-level productivity gains are not automatically converting to organizational value, making pure headcount-reduction plays potentially premature.
insights.quote: 87% of workers now use AI. 73% say it makes them more productive. And on average, they report saving 13 hours per week, a third of a full work week. And yet only 13% say their organization is performing significantly better as a result.

insights.claim: Depending on how time-savings questions are framed, reported savings range from 10 to 14 hours per week; when specifically asked about fully automated work output, the figure settles at approximately 11 hours per week.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Self-reported productivity metrics are highly sensitive to question framing; investors and analysts should discount survey-based AI ROI claims and demand objective telemetry data for verification.

insights.claim: The survey was fielded in December 2025 and January 2026, covering 3,000 US workers, 1,500 in the UK, and 1,500 in Australia — meaning findings reflect a moment just before the latest generation of context-aware models became widely accessible.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Results likely represent a floor for AI productivity potential; model improvements in autonomous context retrieval expected in 2026 could meaningfully shift these numbers in a subsequent wave of the survey.

insights.claim: Approximately 36% of all AI sessions on the Glean platform fail — meaning the worker must start completely from scratch or do significant rework — representing a measurable drag on realized productivity.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: A 36% failure rate is a concrete, objective benchmark for enterprise AI session success; platforms that reduce this rate have a quantifiable value proposition for procurement decisions.
insights.quote: We're seeing about 36% of all AI sessions fail, meaning a worker goes to use the technology and it's not successful.

themes.theme: Bot-sitting: the hidden 6.4-hour/week labor tax on AI adoption

insights.claim: Workers spend an average of 6.4 hours per week on 'bot-sitting' — feeding AI context, overseeing outputs, debugging, and cleaning up errors — consuming roughly half of all reported AI time savings and going largely untracked, unrewarded, and unincentivized.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Any enterprise AI business case that counts raw productivity savings without netting out bot-sitting overhead is overstating ROI by approximately 2x; investors evaluating AI-driven efficiency plays should demand net figures.
insights.quote: It's the feeding AI context. It's the overseeing the AI. It's the debugging the AI. It's the cleaning up after the AI. And it's a massive chunk of labor on average upwards of 6 hours per week.

insights.claim: The highest 'exhaustion multiplier' in bot-sitting comes from two sources: (1) manually feeding AI context that a properly integrated system should already have, and (2) debugging probabilistic LLM outputs where the failure cause is opaque.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Enterprise AI platforms that eliminate manual context injection (via organizational knowledge graphs) and improve output explainability capture the highest marginal value relative to point-solution AI tools.
insights.quote: The highest exhaustion multiplier is associated with feeding AI context right because that is in the best case something your AI should know.

insights.claim: Bot-sitting is correlated with both increased AI success (more bot-sitting associates with more effective AI use) and increased likelihood of leaving the organization, suggesting it simultaneously drives better outcomes and burns out the employees most capable of delivering those outcomes.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Organizations that invest in reducing bot-sitting friction (better-integrated platforms, context graphs) are likely retaining their most productive AI-capable employees as a second-order benefit.

insights.claim: Employees who spend heavy time bot-sitting but whose organization lacks a visible AI strategy are more likely to seek employment elsewhere, interpreting the manual context-feeding burden as evidence that leadership has no coherent AI plan.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: AI tool fragmentation is a hidden retention risk: the top performers doing the most AI work are also the first to read the signal that the org is falling behind and start looking elsewhere.
insights.quote: If you're wasting your days bots sitting and your organization is vocalizing in all hands in town halls that this is a transformative technology and we're transforming there's a disconnect.

themes.theme: Bot-shitting: 69% of workers ship AI work they cannot defend

insights.claim: 69% of surveyed workers admit to some form of 'bot-shitting' — passing off AI-generated work that they cannot explain or defend if questioned — a rate that has risen alongside AI adoption pressure.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: At a 69% rate, uninspected AI outputs are already embedded in organizational workflows; firms without quality-verification infrastructure are accumulating hidden operational and reputational risk at scale.
insights.quote: 69% admit to doing it, a number that reflects both the incredible progress that AIs have made and perhaps the amount of [meaningless] work that people are asked to do.

insights.claim: A separate sub-measure finds 40-41% of employees say they ship AI-generated work that they could not explain if asked — distinguishing a harder core of unverified AI outputs from the broader bot-shitting category.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: 40%+ of knowledge worker outputs may be unverified AI artifacts; this is a material governance and quality-control risk for regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal) that directly affects liability and compliance.
insights.quote: We see 40 to 41% of employees saying they ship AI work that they couldn't explain if asked.

insights.claim: Bot-shitting is driven by a cycle: AI adoption pressure → bot-sitting exhaustion → 'satisficing' (shipping the first good-enough output) → loss of ownership → disengagement, not primarily by individual laziness or bad intent.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Penalizing individuals for bot-shitting without addressing the systemic drivers (tool fragmentation, lack of context, perverse metrics) will not solve the problem and may accelerate turnover of the most AI-capable employees.
insights.quote: The bot sitting is a precursor to the bot [shitting]. It's the exhaustion and employees hit a breaking point.

insights.claim: Bot-shitting is also correlated with employees being on the active job market, and the hypothesis is that extensive bot-shitting reflects broader organizational disengagement, with some high performers leaving because their market value has increased beyond what their current employer offers.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Bot-shitting metrics can serve as a leading indicator of both quality risk and upcoming talent loss; organizations need a dual monitoring approach — output quality scoring plus employee engagement.

insights.claim: The most common visible form of bot-shitting is 'polished nonsense' — outputs that look finished and professional but are substantively hollow — which can pass quality filters precisely because AI is good at surface formatting.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Standard quality review processes built around formatting and completeness will systematically miss bot-shitting; organizations need substantive content evaluation, not just style checks.
insights.quote: It looks polished, it looks finished, it looks like we've done the assignment. When we dive a little bit deeper, it's actually quite hollow.

themes.theme: Coordination neglect and the AI slop hamster wheel

insights.claim: Individual AI productivity gains fail to translate to organizational improvement partly due to 'coordination neglect': one worker expands a bullet into a 5-page AI report, the next worker collapses it back to a bullet, both look productive, but net organizational value created is zero.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Measuring AI ROI at the individual output level (tokens generated, tasks completed) will systematically overstate organizational value; enterprise ROI measurement must account for cross-team coordination efficiency.
insights.quote: Each person looks productive. Each person looks like they've saved massive amounts of time. But when you put the pieces together, it's just this hamster wheel of AI slop.

insights.claim: A substantial share of employees hide their AI usage from managers — specifically because disclosing time savings risks being assigned 6 more hours of work — creating an informal prisoner's dilemma that prevents organizations from capturing aggregate AI gains.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Incentive structures that treat AI-generated time savings as 'slack to be filled' will suppress adoption transparency and prevent organizations from reallocating productivity gains toward higher-value work.
insights.quote: You tell your manager or your organization you're getting 6 hours back probably you're going to then get 6 hours more of work and that is the wrong calculus.

insights.claim: Measuring AI adoption by token consumption or tool-click counts — a practice still observed in organizations — is easily gamed and creates perverse incentives that reward symbolic rather than substantive AI use.
insights.speaker: Nathan Labenz
insights.so_what: Investors and operators should scrutinize AI ROI metrics reported by enterprises; click/token-based metrics are vanity figures that often mask the bot-shitting dynamic.
insights.quote: You could do a lot worse than as a leader just watching your token consumption, but definitely don't tell the team that's how you're going to be measuring them.

insights.claim: AI adoption pressure causes employees who fear displacement to automate the parts of work they are most familiar with — which are often the parts they find most meaningful — creating an alienation dynamic that predicts both reduced engagement and increased turnover.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Blanket automation mandates without role-level meaning analysis will disproportionately destroy the intrinsic motivation of frontline workers most threatened by AI, degrading retention and performance simultaneously.
insights.quote: The natural knee-jerk reaction in too many cases is to automate as much as possible. And unfortunately, in many cases, if you're looking to automate and visibly show that you're transforming with the technology, you're probably going to point it at parts of work that you are most familiar with.

themes.theme: Enterprise context graphs as the structural solution

insights.claim: Glean positions its enterprise platform as a contextual layer connecting people, documents, tasks, goals, and organizational missions — allowing AI to route tasks intelligently across models and provide personalized, proactive recommendations rather than generic answers.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: The competitive moat in enterprise AI shifts from model quality to organizational context depth; platforms that own the enterprise knowledge graph have a defensible position as models commoditize.
insights.quote: When AI is able to truly understand your work, your team's work, and the organization's work, well, then you avoid generic answers and you start to get into this really exciting territory of predictive AI and proactive AI.

insights.claim: The enterprise context graph can enable dynamic, real-time project staffing recommendations — identifying the optimal combination of employees across thousands of people based on skills, bandwidth, career ambitions, and organizational goals — replacing the static org chart for resource allocation.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Dynamic AI-driven staffing is a concrete enterprise workflow automation target with measurable ROI; vendors offering this capability to large enterprises (2,000–10,000+ employees) have a significant addressable market.
insights.quote: Who across your 1,000, 2,000, 10,000 employee base is the right mix of people based on skill sets based on expertise based on career ambitions and passions bandwidth and do a complex calculus that we as humans could never do. That is in my opinion the northstar of enterprise AI.

insights.claim: A healthcare CHRO used AI to task-map all roles across the organization and found that 70% of all tasks overlap with tasks in other roles — indicating that AI-informed role consolidation could be structurally rational at scale for large enterprises.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: 70% task-overlap across roles in a large healthcare org implies significant headcount reduction potential from AI-enabled role rebundling; healthcare, a labor-intensive sector, may face structural downsizing pressure faster than expected.
insights.quote: They've used AI to task map all their roles and just determined that 70% of all tasks are overlapping with other roles across roles.

insights.claim: Glean's telemetry shows that cross-functional AI adoption creates network effects: the likelihood of effective AI use increases significantly when a manager, teammate, or cross-functional colleague also adopts the platform — suggesting platform-level value compounds with breadth of adoption.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: For enterprise AI platforms with strong network effects, the unit economics improve sharply once adoption crosses departmental silos; this argues for broad (not pilot-only) deployment strategies to realize full value.
insights.quote: The network effects associated with this technology are massive.

insights.claim: A large majority of employees report their organizations are already using AI to inform performance management, hiring, and firing — with employee expectations pointing to even higher AI involvement in these HR decisions going forward.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: AI-driven HR decision tools are already in active enterprise use at scale, not just pilot phase; vendors in performance management analytics and HR tech should expect accelerating enterprise procurement demand.

insights.claim: The most effective 13% of organizations — those where employees report significant performance improvements from AI — share a pattern of measuring more things and, crucially, putting that productivity data disproportionately in the hands of employees rather than just managers.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Transparency of AI performance data to employees (not just leadership) is a distinguishing practice of high-performing AI organizations; enterprise platforms that surface individual and team dashboards to end-users have a structural advantage.
insights.quote: In the most effective organizations ... they measure a lot of different things in particular they don't just measure productivity but they also put that data disproportionately more in the hands of employees.

themes.theme: AI cost escalation and the multimodel enterprise architecture

insights.claim: Executives are frequently blowing annual AI token budgets in a single month — AI compute costs are not decreasing for enterprises in the near term — contrary to the narrative that model cost deflation will make AI economics straightforward.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: AI cost overruns are a real P&L risk for enterprises that have committed to broad deployment; the ability to intelligently route tasks to the right model by cost/complexity is a hard economic necessity, not a nice-to-have.
insights.quote: You speak with executives every week where they've blown their AI budgets in a very short time for the year.

insights.claim: The future enterprise AI architecture will be multimodel and multi-tool, with an AI routing layer that directs tasks to the appropriate model based on efficiency, complexity, and cost — not a single-model or single-vendor environment.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Pure-play single-model enterprise AI contracts will commoditize; the durable value lies in the orchestration and routing layer — a structural advantage for platforms like Glean that sit above individual models.
insights.quote: There's no situation in my mind that I can envision where we have a single model, you know, a single tool environment. Employees want that choice.

themes.theme: Org design: flattening, role rebundling, and mission as boss

insights.claim: Jensen Huang / Nvidia's practice of no 1-on-1 meetings with direct reports works because of mission clarity — employees navigate uncertainty by reference to mission rather than managerial hierarchy — but this model cannot be copy-pasted onto organizations without equivalent mission buy-in.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Organizations benchmarking against Nvidia's management practices without equivalent mission clarity will fail; mission alignment is the prerequisite, not an optional soft feature, for de-layered org structures.
insights.quote: What works for Jensen and Nvidia doesn't work for 99.9% of other organizations. Part of it is mission as boss, right?

insights.claim: Organizational flattening (including AI-driven headcount reductions) only works sustainably if employees can navigate uncertainty through mission clarity; without mission, hierarchy removal triggers dysfunction because hierarchy was the source of uncertainty resolution.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: CEOs executing AI-driven delayering without investing in mission communication face elevated execution risk; the organizational design literature provides a testable predictor of which restructurings will succeed or fail.
insights.quote: You can't flatten well if employees don't understand the mission. Because what you're starting to do with job cuts, but also just flattening in general, is you're dismantling hierarchy. And hierarchy gives employees a sense of what to do in situations of uncertainty.

insights.claim: Roles are rebundling in AI-native environments: tasks previously split across 2–4 roles are being consolidated into single roles as AI handles the coordination overhead, creating specialist-to-generalist career path shifts across the economy.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Investors in workforce training, reskilling, and HR tech should expect sustained demand as role rebundling creates large-scale job redefinition across knowledge-worker industries.
insights.quote: I saw the org chart pressure tested and organizations start to think about okay where can AI give me insights across silos in a way that now makes sense to redraw roles that previously lived in two three four different roles now in a single role.

insights.claim: Smaller teams will become the norm as AI reduces the coordination tax of work; fewer managers will be needed as a core managerial function — managing coordination — is automated, but this requires parallel investment in mission and self-directed employee judgment.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Middle management layers are structurally at risk across enterprises; companies should expect continued management delayering as an ongoing trend, not a one-time event, with implications for HR vendor demand and labor market structure.
insights.quote: We should probably need fewer managers if core part of the managerial role has been to manage this coordination tax that can now be taken on with AI.

insights.claim: A Stanford study found that 41% of Y Combinator AI startups are building products that automate things people would prefer to keep human — suggesting a significant portion of AI startup value creation may face latent demand resistance and churn risk.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: AI startups automating 'meaningful work' face a stealth demand problem; investors should probe whether the workflow being automated is one users want automated or one they are merely acquiescing to due to top-down mandate.
insights.quote: 41% of Y Combinator AI startups are automating things that people would prefer to keep human.

themes.theme: AI detection, psychological safety, and talent retention plays

insights.claim: AI content detectors are improving — Pangram Labs is cited as achieving notable precision — but their reliability is enhanced significantly when they can be calibrated against an individual's baseline writing style rather than generic AI patterns.
insights.speaker: Nathan Labenz / Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Enterprise AI detection tools that integrate with organizational knowledge graphs (capturing individual writing fingerprints) have a structural advantage over generic detectors; this is an emerging product wedge in enterprise governance.

insights.claim: The best organizational response to bot-shitting is not crackdown but understanding the 'why' — typically the existing tech stack is not working for high performers who are the most likely to use shadow/unsanctioned AI tools.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Organizations that ban shadow AI usage without diagnosing the underlying tool inadequacy will lose their highest-performing employees to competitors that have better-integrated AI stacks.
insights.quote: Usually it's the highest performers that are doing that. And usually they're doing that because the existing tech stack isn't working for them.

insights.claim: High-performing organizations are up to 5.5x more likely than lower-performing ones to measure and reward effective collaboration — and AI creates an acute version of this challenge as individual productivity gains outrun collective ones.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Collaboration-measurement and collective-reward mechanisms are a distinctive practice of durable high performers; enterprise platforms that make collaboration visible and measurable (via telemetry) have a governance ROI argument beyond individual productivity.
insights.quote: High performing organizations, they're up to 5.5 times more likely than lower effective ones in measuring and rewarding effective collaboration.

insights.claim: Effective organizations are differentiating their AI hackathon incentives: awarding prizes not only for biggest business impact but also for best before-and-after prompts, co-creation feedback, and peer feedback — rewarding collective rather than individual AI productivity.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Incentive design for AI adoption is a nascent organizational practice that is measurably correlated with effective AI transformation; consultants and HR tech vendors offering AI incentive design frameworks have an underserved market.
insights.quote: They also give prizes for improvement, best before and after prompt. They give prizes for co-creation feedback, pure feedback.

insights.claim: Meaningful monetary or structural compensation for employees who excel at AI collaboration is not yet happening at scale in enterprises — it is occurring only in pockets — despite strong evidence that rewarding collective AI performance predicts better organizational outcomes.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: There is a first-mover advantage for enterprises that proactively compensate top AI collaborators; talent markets for 'AI-effective generalists' are not yet pricing this skill premium, creating a retention arbitrage opportunity for early movers.
insights.quote: Should they [be rewarded]? Yes. [But] not in a meaningful way.

insights.claim: Digital exhaustion — employees being worn down by their digital tools and AI interfaces — is strongly predictive of voluntary turnover, per research by Paul Leonardi; as AI creates more bot-sitting friction, digital exhaustion becomes a structurally larger attrition driver.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: The UX and integration quality of enterprise AI tools has direct attrition costs; HR leaders should include digital exhaustion metrics alongside standard engagement surveys to detect AI-driven attrition risk early.
insights.quote: If employees are exhausted by technology, they're wanting to leave the organization.

insights.claim: Companies that have rapidly cut human customer service agents via AI and then discovered those agents were doing relationship-building work that AI could not replicate have had to rehire — illustrating that hasty AI-driven headcount reductions carry reversal risk and hidden costs.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: AI-driven workforce reductions in customer-facing roles have a non-trivial reversal rate; companies that use enterprise graph analytics to map the actual relationship value of human agents before automating will avoid costly rehiring cycles.
insights.quote: You cut the human agents you realize wow those human agents did a really important work in building long-term relationships with customers. You then bring them back.

insights.claim: AI-native companies have a structural advantage over legacy organizations in AI transformation because they lack the existing org chart baggage, legacy tooling, and incumbent culture — but legacy firms should not copy-paste AI-native business models onto their existing structures.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: The competitive gap between AI-native entrants and legacy incumbents will widen if legacy firms waste resources mimicking AI-native structures rather than AI-enabling their existing core competencies; sector-level disruption risk is highest in labor-intensive legacy industries.
insights.quote: What works for an AI native company does not work for a legacy company. I think there's going to be two models.

insights.claim: The top-down change management model (mandates, memos, CEO demos) is necessary but insufficient; the most effective organizations pair it with bottom-up activation — identifying and empowering 'AI influencers' or champions within teams to drive meaningful rather than symbolic adoption.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: Enterprise AI vendors and consultants whose deployment approach relies exclusively on top-down executive sponsorship are structurally underperforming relative to those that build grassroots champion programs; this is a differentiating go-to-market competency.
insights.quote: The best organizations, the most effective ones, they're having a bifurcated strategy. Yes. Top down change is important. But bottom-up change is just as important.

insights.claim: Glean plans to evolve the Work AI Index into a pulse survey updated every six months, enabling longitudinal tracking of bot-sitting rates, role evolution, and organizational AI performance over time.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Hinds
insights.so_what: A biannual enterprise AI health index from a platform with objective telemetry will become a meaningful market benchmark; investors and analysts following enterprise AI adoption should track this dataset as a forward indicator.
